coredata.dc:title: A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine detection based on FRET between quantum dots and graphene oxide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Pu
Luo, Chen
Chen, Xiaoxiao
Huang, Chaobiao
coredata.dc:description: A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was established for adenosine detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CdS QDs, DNA QDs as donor and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor. Amino-riched DNA QDs covalently bonded to the carboxyl group on the edge of the GO, and with the absorption of the TGA-modified CdS QDs with aptamer (CdS QDs-apt) onto the GO surface via the π-π stacking interaction. The fluorescence of both CdS QDs and DNA QDs were efficiently quenched due to FRET (turn off). When adenosine was present, the specific binding of the aptamer to the target preferentially that released the CdS QDs-apt from GO. The process would inhibit the FRET which contribute to the fluorescence of CdS QDs-apt recovery again (turn on), while the fluorescence intensity of DNA QDs only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal. Thus, a novel “off-on“ ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine detection was constructed accordingly. There was a good linearity relationship between the ratio of the FL intensity (F595 nm/F464 nm) and the concentration of adenosine in the range of 20.00–180.0 nmol/L with a detection limit of 1.3 nmol/L (S/N = 3, n = 9). Importantly, the feasibility of the developed aptasensor for selective detection of adenosine in serum and urine samples with satisfactory results. The recoveries were observed to be 97.04–100.2 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor
Adenosine
FRET
Quantum dots
Graphene oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdS QDs and CdS QDs-apt
Systhesis of DNA QDs and DNA QDs/GO
Characterization of the prepared DNA QDs
Characterization of CdS QDs and CdS QDs-apt
Effect of apt and GO concentrations on fluorescence of CdS QDs
Effect of incubation time between DNA QDs/GO and CdS QDs-apt, AD and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt
Analytical performance of the aptasensor for detection of AD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present research was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21575129 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A) and particle size distribution (B) of DNA QDs.
XPS spectrum of DNA QDs (A) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s (D).
(A) FTIR spectrum of DNA QDs. (B) Absorption spectrum of DNA QDs.
TEM and particle size distribution images of CdS QDs (A, C) and CdS QDs-apt (B, D).
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of apt (a), CdS QDs (b) and CdS QDs-apt (c). (B) XPS spectra of CdS QDs (a) and CdS QDs-apt (b). Inset: P 2p XPS spectra of CdS QDs (a)and CdS QDs-apt (b).
TEM images of GO (A), DNA QDs/GO (B), DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt (C).
(A) FTIR spectra of GO (a) and DNA QDs/GO (b) (B) XPS spectra of DNA QDs/GO (a) and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt (b). Inset: Cd 3d XPS spectra of DNA QDs/GO (a) and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt(b).
UV–vis absorption spectrum of GO (a), emission spectra of DNA QDs (b) and CdS QDs (c).
The formation process of DNA QDs.
The sensing mechanism of the proposed DNA/GO/CdS QDs-apt FRET switch for AD detection.

Optimization of apt concentrations (A), GO concentrations (B), incubation time of CdS QDs-apt and DNA QDs/GO (C), incubation pH (D), incubation time between AD and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt €, incubation temperature (F).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor with the increase of AD concentrations (λ ex = 400 nm and λ em = 464/593 nm). (B) The linear correlation of fluorescent intensity ratio. (F595/F464) with AD concentrations from 0 to 200 nM.
Selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor.
Comparison of other analysis methods of AD detection.
The content and recovery of AD in serum and urine samples.

head.ce:title: A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine detection based on FRET between quantum dots and graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xingzhi College, Zhejiang Normal University, Lanxi 321100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xingzhi College, Zhejiang Normal University, Lanxi 321100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Xingzhi College, Zhejiang Normal University, Lanxi 321100, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensing mechanism of the proposed DNA/GO/CdS QDs-apt FRET switch for AD detection.

A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was established for adenosine detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CdS QDs, DNA QDs as donor and graphene oxide (GO) as acceptor. Amino-riched DNA QDs covalently bonded to the carboxyl group on the edge of the GO, and with the absorption of the TGA-modified CdS QDs with aptamer (CdS QDs-apt) onto the GO surface via the π-π stacking interaction. The fluorescence of both CdS QDs and DNA QDs were efficiently quenched due to FRET (turn off). When adenosine was present, the specific binding of the aptamer to the target preferentially that released the CdS QDs-apt from GO. The process would inhibit the FRET which contribute to the fluorescence of CdS QDs-apt recovery again (turn on), while the fluorescence intensity of DNA QDs only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal. Thus, a novel “off-on“ ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine detection was constructed accordingly. There was a good linearity relationship between the ratio of the FL intensity (F595 nm/F464 nm) and the concentration of adenosine in the range of 20.00–180.0 nmol/L with a detection limit of 1.3 nmol/L (S/N = 3, n = 9). Importantly, the feasibility of the developed aptasensor for selective detection of adenosine in serum and urine samples with satisfactory results. The recoveries were observed to be 97.04–100.2 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine (AD) as an endogenous nucleoside performs extremely important roles in signal transduction in biological processes such as anti-arrhythmia, vasodilatation, increasing arterial blood flow and improving myocardial oxygen supply [1–3]. In addition, AD has an inhibitory effect on synaptic activity and slows down the metabolic activity of the brain, and may also plays a significant role in the immune system [4,5]. Increasing evidence suggests that excessive AD may indicate tumorigenesis, especially in lung cancer [6–8]. Therefore, direct monitoring of AD fluctuations under physiological conditions would be of utility in further elucidating its role in cancer clinical diagnosis and treatment.

Up to date, several analytical strategies for detecting AD are the colorimetry [7], electrochemical methods [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], surface enhanced Raman scattering [12] and resonance light scattering [13]. Even through these strategies are effective, these methods are time-consuming and require sophisticated instruments. Recently, with the development of biosensor technology, fluorescence aptasensors with high sensitivity and selectivity have attracted extensive attention in AD detection [5,14,15] However, quantitative target analysis using fluorescent probes with single emission characteristics can be interfered by various factors unrelated to the analyte (such as instrumental parameters, background interference and detection environment) [16,17]. Ratiometric fluorescence analysis that measures the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths can reduce or even eliminate erroneous signals caused by environmental changes to the largest extent. So, ratiometric fluorescence as an important and powerful analytical approach has attracted widespread consideration for the quantitative determination of the trace amounts of targets [18,19]. So far, ratiometric fluorescence analyses have been designed for AD detection. Liu et al developed a ratiometric nanoprobe for biosensing based on green fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets as an internal reference and quenching platform for detection of AD [20].

Aptamers (apt), which are artificial nucleic acids, exhibit many additional merits such as easy to produce and label, possess good storage properties and high resistance to denaturation, and high binding affinity with target (peptides, proteins, small organic molecules, or ions), thus facilitating their incorporation into biosensor fabrication [21,22]. Their combination with the targets will result in the change of its three-dimensional folding structure, forming the hairpin, stem-loop, G-quadruplex, pseudoknot or bulge structures [23,24]. Huizenga et al. indicated that the complex structure between ssDNA-aptamer and adenosine in solution consists of two small Watson-Crick helices and two G-quartets [14,25].

QDs have been widely used as ideal fluorescent probes for signal generation and transduction in the fluorescent sensing of analytes [26,27]. Especially, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (CdS QDs) are excellent donors in fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect (FRET) with excellent properties such as high light stability, high quantum yield, good biocompatibility, easy synthesis and surface modification. Besides, DNA QDs as novel fluorescent probes not only have a luminescent behavior similar to CQDs, but it also owns a DNA-like structure [28,29], which have great application prospects in many fields such as biosensing and bioimaging [28,30].

Among various excellent energy acceptors employed to fabricate FRET sensors, graphene oxide (GO) has attracted extensive attention in fluorescence quenching of various semiconductor QDs due to its large area conjugate structure, easy surface modification and higher quenching efficiency [31,32]. Furthermore, GO can adsorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) by hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions between the hexagonal cells of the GO and nucleotide bases [33–36]. Therefore, CdS QDs can be assembled on GO by π-π stacking after binding with the aptamer.

In the current study, a sensitive and specific ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor based on FRET between CdS QDs, DNA QDs as donor's species and GO as acceptor has been developed for AD detection. Firstly, CdS QDs and DNA QDs were combined with aptamer and GO to form precursors (CdS QDs-apt and DNA-GO) through amidation reaction using EDC/NHS as activator, respectively. Thereafter, CdS QDs-apt adsorbed onto GO via π-π stacking interaction. Consequently, the fluorescence of both DNA QDs and CdS QDs were efficiently quenched by GO. When AD was present, CdS QDs-apt which preferentially bound to the target AD release from the GO and the fluorescence of CdS QDs turns “on” again, while the fluorescence intensity of DNA QDs was only slightly altered. The ratio of FL intensity (CdS QDs/DNA QDs, F595/F464) increased with the increase of AD concentration.

Sodium sulfide (Na2S), Tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), Chromic chloride (CdCl2), Guanine (Gua), Adenine (Ade), Thymine (Thy), Cytosine (Cyt), Cysteine (Cys), Arginine (Arg), Histidine (His), Alanine (Ala), Urea were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), Mercaptoacetic acid (TGA) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd., USA; Salmon extract low molecular weight double stranded DNA were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Adenosine (AD), Guanosine GS, Cytidine (CD) was from Huawei Ruike Chemical Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Amino-modified aptamer: 5′-NH2C6-ACC TGG GGG AGT ATT GCG GAG GAA GGT-3′ were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China); All of the aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water throughout the whole experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with Lambda 950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). The morphological evaluations were characterized by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded using RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was conducted on ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Reonicolai Inc., USA). Fluorescence lifetime spectra are determined by FLS 980 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Tenmei, Edinburgh). Plus-E3-20TH Ultrapure Water Device (Nieolet, USA). PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Yidian Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.), 1810D Electronic Analytical Balance (Saidoris, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS QDs and CdS QDs-apt
ce:section.ce:para: Functionalized CdS QDs were synthesized as previous report [37]. 250 µL TGA was added to 50 mL 0.01 mol/L CdCl2 solution in a 150 mL tri-neck flask and the mixture was adjust pH to 11.0 with a constant stirring by the drop wise addition of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) and removed oxygen through N2 for 30 min. Then added 5.0 mL 0.1 mol/L Na2S solution and refluxed at 100 ℃ for 4 h under the protection of nitrogen. The mother liquor of CdS QDs were obtained and kept at 4 °C for further usage.

Apt was modified to the surface of CdS QDs by EDC/NHS coupling reaction. Briefly, 1 mL of CdS QDs mother liquor were mixed with 400 µL EDC (0.002 g/mL) and 200 µL NHS (0.002 g/mL) in PBS buffer (0.1 mol/L, pH 7.4). Subsequently, 100 µL of apt (2 μmol/L) was added to the mixture and reacted at 4 °C for 12 h in refrigerator. Unbound apt and CdS QDs were removed by centrifugation and the precipitates were washed three times with 2 mL PBS buffer. The CdS QDs-apt were therefore obtained and dispersed with 2 mL PBS buffer for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Systhesis of DNA QDs and DNA QDs/GO
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of DNA QDs was modified as previous report [38]. Salmon sodium deoxyribonucleic acid was dissolved in ultrapure water which had been removed oxygen through N2 for 30 min to obtain the homogeneous DNA solution. Then the DNA solution was refluxed at 85 °C for 11 h with the protection of N2. Finally, it was quickly added to the same volume of acetone being sonicated. After ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, the mixed solution was removed acetone by rotary evaporation. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the DNA QDs solution and kept at 4 °C for further use.

1 mL of the GO (0.4 mg/mL) solution was activated with 1 mL of EDC (0.002 g/mL) and 1 mL NHS (0.002 g/mL) for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of DNA QDs were added above mixture and reacted at 4 °C for 12 h in refrigerator. The mixture was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and the precipitates were washed three times with 2 mL PBS buffer and then dispersed with 2 mL PBS buffer for further use.

CdS QDs-apt (200 µL) and DNA QDs/GO (300 µL) were mixed and incubated at 37° C in thermostatic culture oscillator for 110 min. Then different concentrations of adenosine (100 µL) were added into above mixed solution and continued to incubate for 20 min. The mixture was adjusted to 1 mL with PBS buffer. Its fluorescence spectra and variation of fluorescence ratio values (F595/F464) were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

Human serum and urine sample (received from Jinhua City Central Hospital, China) were pretreated with a simple centrifuge and then diluted 100 times. The supernatant was filtered by 0.22 µm porous membrane for fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared DNA QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-synthesized DNA QDs was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) in Fig. 1 A. It can be clearly seen that the DNA QDs exhibited spherical and well-dispersed with average diameter at about 2.5 nm (Fig. 1B).

The elemental compositions and surface states of DNA QDs was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 A, there were four characteristic peaks observed at 284.24, 404.33, 530.67 and 132.52 eV were considered as the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and P 2p binding peak, respectively [30,38,39]. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1s peaks (Fig. 2B) at 285.03, 286.27, 287.92 and 286.65 eV can be assigned to carbon in the form of CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively [40–44]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) can be de-convoluted into two characteristic peaks at 405.01 and 404.70 eV, which was attributed to N and NH2 [38,45]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) indicated the main peaks at 530.80, 531.51, 532.16 and 532.61 eV corresponding to
PO
4
1-
, CO, ON and CO, respectively [39,46]. The obtained results indicated that the synthesized DNA QDs was rich in nitrogenous and oxygen-containing groups and owned the original phosphate skeleton of DNA.

Fig. 3 A showed FTIR spectrum of DNA QDs and analysed the surface’s functional groups. The peaks at 1036 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C-N in-plane stretching vibration [30,47,48], which indicated the existence of amine groups in prepared DNA QDs. The observed peaks at 1481, 1377 and 1711 cm−1 correspond to CO deformation vibration, COH tensile vibration and CO stretching vibration [46,49]. Mentioned FTIR result suggests that the DNA QDs own COOH, OH functional groups on the surface.

Fig. 3B displayed the UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of DNA QDs. Curve a showed a strong peak at 265 nm which was similar to the characteristic absorption peak of DNA and could be attributed to the presence of delocalized π electron from heterocyclic rings of purines and pyrimidines in DNA [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CdS QDs and CdS QDs-apt
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 displayed the TEM and particle size distribution images of CdS QDs (A, C) and CdS QDs-apt (B, D). CdS QDs and CdS QDs-apt were spherical or ellipsoid and the particle size distribution of CdS QDs-apt had slightly increased which indirectly demonstrated that apt have been combined with CdS QDs successfully.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of apt (a), CdS QDs (b) and CdS QDs-apt (c) were investigated in Fig. 5 A, the characteristic absorption peak of apt and CdS QDs are at 254 and 420 nm, respectively. The same peaks at 254 and 420 nm of CdS QDs-apt were observed that denoting the successful immobilization of apt onto CdS QDs.

Fig. 5B showed XPS spectra of CdS QDs (a), CdS QDs-apt (b). The peaks around 405, 160, 285, 404 and 530 eV that can be clearly observed the characteristic peaks of Cd 3d, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Additionally, high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p before and after apt binding of CdS QDs were analyzed in inset of Fig. 5B. Comparing curves a and b, it can be found that CdS QDs-apt added a P 2p peak at 132.7 ev, indicated that the apt had been successfully bound to the surface of CdS QDs [22,51].

TEM images of the GO (A), DNA QDs/GO (B) and DNA QDs/CdS QDs-apt/GO (C) were shown in Fig. 6 . As can be seen in Fig. 6A, it clearly exhibited the sheet-like microstructures, occasional folds, wrinkles and ultrathin features of GO. Fig. 6B showed that DNA QDs were preferentially immobilized onto the GO edge by covalent interaction because of high density of –COOH groups located there. Fig. 6C displayed TEM image of the DNA QDs/CdS QDs-apt/GO that proved the π-π stacking interaction between the aromatic bases of the apt and the hexagonal cells of GO. In addition, the FTIR and XPS spectra were employed to investigate immobilization of DNA QDs and CdS QDs-apt onto the GO. As shown in Fig. 7 A, compared with curve a and b, the characteristic absorption peaks appear at 1641 cm−1 and 1516 cm−1 indicating the presence of amide vibration, which confirms the successfully formation of the amide bond between GO and DNA QDs [52,53]. Fig. 7B showed XPS images of DNA QDs/GO (a) and DNA QDs/CdS QDs-apt/GO (b). The characteristic peaks of N 1s, S 2p, C 1s and O 1s can be clearly observed near 404, 168, 286 and 531 eV, respectively. Additionally, high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d before and after DNA QDs/GO combined with CdS QDs-apt were analyzed in inset of Fig. 7B. It can be clearly discovered that Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2 peaks at 404.19 and 410.89 eV for DNA QDs/CdS QDs-apt/GO, indicated the assembly of CdS QDs-apt onto DNA QDs/GO.

As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the GO has a broad absorption band in the UV–vis region, which has well overlapped with the emission of DNA QDs and CdS QDs, thus provided a reliable foundation for FRET to occur from QDs to GO. The formation process of DNA QDs was shown in Fig. 9 , single-stranded DNA was produced by simply hydrothermal treatment of deoxyribonucleic acid, then the aromatic cytosine bases in the single strand of DNA can spontaneously accumulate to form the sp2 carbon-like centers. The highly hydrophilic PO4 1- group on the skeleton was located outside the center of the sp2 carbon, which wraps around the cencer of the sp2 carbon to form DNA QDs [54]. DNA QDs were attached to the edge of GO to form DNA QDs/GO, and then the CdS QDs-apt and DNA QDs/GO readily form stable DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt complex via strong π-π stacking interaction. Resulting in the occurrence of FRET and the fluorescence of both CdS QDs and DNA QDs were efficiently quenched by GO. In the presence of AD, specifically interaction between AD and CdS QDs-apt led to the release of the CdS QDs-apt from GO and the recovery of the fluorescence of CdS QDs, while the fluorescence intensity of DNA QDs was only slightly altered and acted as the reference signal in ratiometric fluorescence assay. Thus, a novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for AD detection based on FRET between QDs and GO had been established (Fig. 10 ).

To achieve the excellent analytical performance, critical parameters such as the concentration of apt and GO, incubation time between DNA QDs/GO and CdS QDs-apt and between AD and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt, pH and temperature were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of apt and GO concentrations on fluorescence of CdS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: When the apt concentration increased from 0 to 2 μmol/L, the fluorescence of CdS QDs-apt was gradually enhanced, probably because the surface defects of the CdS QDs were reduced [55]. When the apt concentration increased from 2 μmol/L to 5 μmol/L, the fluorescence of CdS QDs-apt was gradually weakened (Fig. 11 A), this may be because the excessively long chain structure of the apt blocked the fluorescence site, leading to the reduction of the absorbed excitation light, and thus the fluorescence efficiency was reduced [56]. Thus, 2 μmol/L was selected as the optimal concentration of apt. Furthermore, with increasing the concentration of GO, the fluorescence quenching rate was increased notably and tended to balance until it reached the 0.4 mg/mL (Fig. 1B). Thus, 0.5 mg/mL of GO was selected as the definitive concentration in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time between DNA QDs/GO and CdS QDs-apt, AD and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11C showed the fluorescence intensity of CdS QDs with the incubation time between DNA QDs/GO and CdS QDs-apt. It can be seen that fluorescence intensity was almost reached the lostest when the incubation time reaches 100 min. Therefore, 110 min was chosen as the incubation time. Fig. 11D exhibited that the optimization of incubation time between AD and DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt. The obtained result illustrated that the optimal incubation time was 20 min. F2 and F1 were the fluorescence intensity of CdS QDs-apt in the presence and absence of AD, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 11E (a), the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of the DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt without AD was investigated over the pH range 5.7–9.0 and significant changes were not observed in the fluorescence intensity ratio of DNA QDs/GO/CdS QDs-apt (F595/F464). Fig. 11E (b) showed the influence of AD on F595/F464 at different pH values and F595/F464 reached the maximum value at pH 7.4. Probably because that the higher or lower pH environments may damage the stability and activity of immobilized biomolecules and then influence the lifetime of aptasensors [55,56]. Therefore, 0.1 mol/L PBS buffer at pH 7.4 was applied for subsequent experiment.
Fig. 11F exhibited that the optimization temperature. The obtained result illustrated that the optimal temperature was 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the aptasensor for detection of AD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 A showed the fluorescent spectra of ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor exposed to the different concentration of AD perform at excitation wavelength 400 nm. The results displayed that the increase of AD concentration to the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor indeed induces an obvious fluorescence enhancement at 595 nm whereas the emission intensity at 464 nm was still unchanged. Consequently, the emission intensity ratio (F595/F464) was gradually increased with increase of AD concentrations. The linear regression equations for AD detection were shown in Fig. 12B, demonstrated that a good linear relationship of the emission intensity ratio (F595/F464) with the concentration of AD in the range of 20.00 to 180.0 nmol/L. The linear equation was y = 0.017 x + 0.872 (R2 = 0.9970) and the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.3 nmol/L (S/N = 3, n = 9).

The ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was used to detect AD in human serum and urine. As can be seen in Table 2 , the results of AD content in human serum and urine were 3.60 and 4.91 μM, respectively, and satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the range of 97.04–100.2 %.

In conclusion, the ratiometric aptasensor via FRET between CdS QDs and DNA QDs as donor's and GO as acceptor has been developed for detection of AD and has good selectivity for AD. In general, the concentration of AD in normal human is in the range of 2.0–7.0 μM while the results of AD content we measured in human serum and urine were 3.60 and 4.91 μM. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor can be used for the selective detection of AD in real samples, and it can be developed as novel ratiometric fluorescence assay for analysis of other biomolecules or biomarkers and diagnostics of related diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present research was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575129).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor based on bipolar electrode for detection of adenosine in cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.W. Shi, M.S. Wu, Y. Du, J.J. Xu, H.Y. Chen, Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor based on bipolar electrode for detection of adenosine in cancer cells, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 55 (2014) 459-463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine based on the use of quaternary CuInZnS quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, S. Chen, T. Hu, Q. Ma, Fluorescent aptasensor for adenosine based on the use of quaternary CuInZnS quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, Microchimica Acta, 184 (2017) 1361-1367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric detection of guanosine and adenosine using a carbon paste electrode modified with 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Gao, Y. Duan, M. Xi, W. Sun, Voltammetric detection of guanosine and adenosine using a carbon paste electrode modified with 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate, Microchimica Acta, 172 (2010) 57-64.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time monitoring of extracellular adenosine using enzyme-linked microelectrode arrays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Hinzman, J.L. Gibson, R.D. Tackla, M.S. Costello, J.J. Burmeister, J.E. Quintero, G.A. Gerhardt, J.A. Hartings, Real-time monitoring of extracellular adenosine using enzyme-linked microelectrode arrays, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 74 (2015) 512-517.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective and sensitive detection of adenosine utilizing signal amplification based on silver ions-assisted cation exchange reaction with CdTe quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Song, T. Tang, X. Wang, G. Xu, F. Wei, Y. Wu, Q. Song, Y. Ma, Y. Ma, Y. Cen, Q. Hu, Highly selective and sensitive detection of adenosine utilizing signal amplification based on silver ions-assisted cation exchange reaction with CdTe quantum dots, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 247 (2017) 305-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Release of Engineered Nanomaterials from Polymer Nanocomposites: the Effect of Matrix Degradation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: G-quadruplex based Exo III-assisted signal amplification aptasensor for the colorimetric detection of adenosine
